title: Kernel Fusion in NVIDIA CUDA: Optimizing Memory Traffic and Launch Overhead | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA demonstrates three complementary ways to fuse GPU kernels (manual CUDA C++, torch.compile/Inductor, and the new cuda.compute Python library built on CUB) that each cut memory traffic 3x on a sum(abs(x)) benchmark, positioning cuda.compute as a middle path offering Python ergonomics with the deterministic, predictable fusion behavior of hand-written CUDA — reinforcing CUDA's software-layer moat beyond raw silicon.

themes.theme: The core bottleneck and the fusion fix

insights.claim: A common GPU bottleneck is that compute is so fast that even high-bandwidth device memory can't keep the kernel fed, so multi-kernel pipelines waste bandwidth round-tripping intermediate results through global memory between separate kernel launches.
insights.speaker: Daniel Rodriguez
insights.so_what: Frames memory bandwidth, not raw FLOPs, as the practical ceiling for many real GPU workloads — relevant context for evaluating GPU vendor benchmark claims that emphasize peak compute over sustained bandwidth-bound throughput.
insights.quote: A common bottleneck when writing GPU code is that GPU compute is so fast that even high-bandwidth device memory doesn’t use the GPU kernel fully.

insights.claim: CUDA Graphs and kernel-body fusion are complementary but distinct optimizations: graphs reduce host-side launch overhead by capturing a sequence of kernel launches into one dispatchable object, but do not eliminate the memory round-trip between separate kernels, which only kernel-body fusion addresses.
insights.speaker: Daniel Rodriguez
insights.so_what: Clarifies that NVIDIA's optimization stack has multiple independent levers (launch overhead vs. memory traffic) that developers must stack together for full performance, illustrating the depth/complexity of the CUDA toolset as a barrier to replicate.
insights.quote: Wrapping a naive baseline in a graph shaves microseconds off the host side, but the one GiB round-trip through the intermediate buffer remains unchanged. The two approaches are complementary and can be used together.

insights.claim: On an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090, manually fusing a two-kernel sum(abs(x)) operation into one kernel cut global memory traffic from 3 GB to 1 GB, cut kernel launches from 2 to 1, and cut execution time from 3.51 ms to 1.18 ms, a roughly 3x speedup, while achieving ~850 GiB/s effective bandwidth (about 90% of the card's 1,008 GB/s theoretical peak).
insights.speaker: Daniel Rodriguez
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that a purely software-level restructuring (no new hardware) can deliver 3x throughput on existing consumer GPUs already in the field, underscoring how much performance headroom is unlocked by software optimization on NVIDIA's installed base.
insights.quote: The memory bandwidth sits at ~850 GiB/s—roughly 90% of its theoretical 1,008 GB/s peak.
insights.locator: Table 1, benchmark for the manually fused kernel

insights.claim: All three fusion methods tested (manual CUDA C++, torch.compile/Inductor, and cuda.compute) converge on the same result: 1 GiB of memory traffic and about a 3x speedup over the unfused naive baseline, despite using very different levels of developer effort and control.
insights.speaker: Daniel Rodriguez
insights.so_what: Suggests the memory-bandwidth ceiling is largely method-agnostic once fusion is achieved, meaning developers can choose the fusion approach that best fits their productivity/control tradeoff without leaving performance on the table.
insights.quote: All three fused methods end up in the same place with 1 GiB of memory traffic and about a 3x speedup over the naive version.
insights.locator: Table 2, summary of kernel fusion methods

themes.theme: Compiler fusion is powerful but unpredictable

insights.claim: torch.compile's Inductor compiler automatically fuses operations into kernels traced from plain Python/PyTorch code, but the exact fusion strategy it chooses (e.g., how many kernels, how reductions are split) is opaque and can silently change with a different dtype, shape, or a small code edit, without warning.
insights.speaker: Daniel Rodriguez
insights.so_what: A key risk callout for teams relying on compiler auto-fusion for production inference/training performance: fusion behavior is not guaranteed stable across code or shape changes, creating hidden performance regression risk that NVIDIA's explicit cuda.compute approach is positioned to solve.
insights.quote: A different dtype, a different shape, or a small operation added in the middle can produce a completely different set of kernels. Sometimes you get one kernel. Sometimes you get four. Sometimes a fusion you were counting on quietly stops happening, without warning.

insights.claim: The compiled PyTorch version splits sum(abs(x)) into two Triton kernels (triton_red_fused_abs_sum_0 and _1) rather than one, because Inductor chose to structure the reduction in two stages — a different strategy than the manual CUDA kernel would use, though both hit the same 1 GiB memory-traffic result.
insights.speaker: Daniel Rodriguez
insights.so_what: Illustrates concretely that compiler-generated kernels are a black box whose internal structure developers don't control, even when end performance matches hand-tuned code — a data point for the buy-vs-build debate in GPU kernel development.
insights.quote: The compiler picked a reasonable strategy but not the same strategy we would choose.

themes.theme: cuda.compute as NVIDIA's new middle-ground offering

insights.claim: cuda.compute is a new NVIDIA Python library that exposes device-wide algorithms (reduce, scan, sort, transform) and composable lazy iterators (e.g., TransformIterator) so developers get explicit, deterministic kernel fusion with Python ergonomics, without writing CUDA C++ directly, and it interoperates with PyTorch tensors, DLPack, and the CUDA Array API (e.g., CuPy).
insights.speaker: Daniel Rodriguez
insights.so_what: Signals NVIDIA's product strategy: extending CUDA's software moat into the Python data-science/ML ecosystem with a library that offers CUB-backed, battle-tested kernels via a Pythonic API, competing directly with compiler-based fusion approaches like torch.compile for developer mindshare.
insights.quote: cuda.compute gives Python developers access to the same iterator-based, deterministic fusion that Thrust and CUB have long provided in C++.
insights.locator: Explicit kernel fusion: Callbacks, iterators, and epilogs

insights.claim: Unlike compiler-driven fusion, cuda.compute's fusion is explicit and deterministic because the developer directly composes the computation passed to the algorithm, and it won't silently change strategy due to a compiler version change or unrelated code edit; it is built on CUB, giving it access to NVIDIA's continually optimized, hardware-specific implementations.
insights.speaker: Daniel Rodriguez
insights.so_what: Positions cuda.compute as reducing a real production risk (silent fusion regressions) while still capturing new-hardware optimizations automatically via CUB, an appealing pitch for ML infrastructure teams choosing their GPU compute stack.
insights.quote: Unlike a compiler approach, it won’t silently change the fusion strategy because of a small code change, a different compiler version, or a compiler decision, because you explicitly decide what should be fused.
insights.locator: Explicit kernel fusion: Callbacks, iterators, and epilogs

insights.claim: The CUDA C++ examples in the post rely on newly introduced CCCL runtime interfaces (cuda::std::span, cuda::launch, cuda::make_buffer) that debuted in CUDA 13.2, indicating NVIDIA is actively modernizing its core C++ GPU programming interfaces.
insights.speaker: Daniel Rodriguez
insights.so_what: A minor but concrete data point on NVIDIA's release cadence for core developer tooling (CCCL/CUDA 13.2), useful for tracking the pace of the software platform's evolution alongside hardware generations.
insights.quote: The C++ examples featured throughout this post use the latest NVIDIA CCCL runtime interfaces, including cuda::std::span, cuda::launch, and cuda::make_buffer—which debuted in CUDA 13.2.
